prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
# Translations

## French
**"Le silence entre nous en disait plus long que n'importe quelle dispute n'aurait pu le faire."**

## Japanese
**「私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな口論よりも多くを物語っていた。」**
*(Watashitachi no aida no chinmoku wa, donna kōron yori mo ōku o monogatatte ita.)*

## Arabic
**"الصمتُ الذي كان بيننا قال أكثر مما كان بإمكان أي جدالٍ أن يقوله."**
*(Aṣ-ṣamtu alladhī kāna baynanā qāla akthara mimmā kāna bi-imkāni ayyi jidālin an yaqūlahu.)*

---

# Translator's Notes

### French — "said more than any argument *could have*"
The English counterfactual ("could have") is tricky because French prefers a fuller conditional construction. I used **"n'aurait pu le faire"** (literally "would not have been able to do it") with the **ne explétif** ("n'en disait plus long... que... n'aurait pu"), which is standard in comparative clauses in careful French. A looser rendering like *"que n'importe quelle dispute"* would sound more casual but slightly less literary.

### Japanese — "said" (spoke volumes)
Japanese doesn't use "say" for silence naturally. I chose **物語っていた (monogatatte ita)**, meaning "was telling a story / was speaking volumes," which captures the figurative weight better than the literal 言う (*iu*, to say). Also, "argument" was rendered as **口論 (kōron)**, implying a verbal quarrel; 議論 (*giron*, debate) would have been too neutral and intellectual.

### Arabic — "between us" and the counterf
